title: Applying the Gradient: Updating Weights
concept: After backprop finds a gradient, each weight is nudged in the opposite direction, and the learning rate decides how big that nudge is.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a gradient tell a neural network during backpropagation?

options.text: How a small weight change would affect the loss.
Which training example should be deleted.
How many new layers to add.

exercises.explain: Correct! A gradient is a sensitivity signal: it tells the model how the loss would change if a weight changed a little.

cards.title: After the miss
cards.body: A robot basketball shooter misses slightly to the right. It does not throw away its whole training history. Instead, it makes a tiny adjustment to the aim controls before the next shot. That tiny correction is what a weight update is: learn, nudge, try again.

cards.title: The update step
cards.body: Backpropagation tells each weight which way would make the loss grow faster. To improve, the model moves the weight the other way. The learning rate controls how big that move is, like choosing whether to take a tiny shuffle or a bigger step.

cards.title: Turning the knob back
cards.body: Imagine a shower knob. If the water is too hot, you do not turn the knob even hotter. You turn it the opposite way, and usually not all at once. The gradient says which direction is making things worse, and the learning rate says how hard to turn.

cards.title: Prediction too high
cards.body: Suppose one weight helps push a prediction upward, and the model keeps guessing too high. Backprop may give that weight a positive gradient, meaning 'increasing this weight tends to increase the loss.' So the update step lowers that weight a little before the next example.

cards.title: Weights are nudged, not rebooted
cards.body: A common myth is that training means replacing weights with brand-new numbers every round. Not true. Most of the time, learning is many small nudges. The network keeps useful patterns and slowly refines them.

cards.title: How the sign changes the move
cards.body: A weight update depends on the gradient's direction and the learning rate's size.

left.text:
Increasing the weight would raise loss.
So the update step moves the weight down a little.

right.text:
Increasing the weight would lower loss.
So the update step moves the weight up a little.

exercises.prompt: What job does the learning rate do during a weight update?

options.text: It sets how big the update step should be.
It stores the activations in memory.
It counts the number of layers.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The learning rate is the step-size dial for how strongly the model reacts to the gradient.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a weight has a positive gradient, the usual update step lowers that weight to try to reduce loss.
exercises.explain: True! A positive gradient means 'moving this weight upward increases loss,' so the model steps downward instead.

exercises.sentence: To reduce loss, the model usually moves in the ___ direction of the gradient.
exercises.explain: The gradient points uphill for loss, so the model usually steps the opposite way to go downhill.

exercises.prompt: Which update sounds like a model correctly applying a gradient?

options.text: A weight gets a positive gradient, so the optimizer decreases that weight a little.
A weight gets a positive gradient, so the optimizer doubles the weight to learn faster.
A weight gets any gradient, so the model resets every weight to 0.

exercises.explain: Correct. Applying the gradient means moving against the uphill direction, not exaggerating it or resetting the network.

exercises.prompt: A music app's model predicts song volume too high for many examples. One important weight gets a positive gradient of 0.4, and the learning rate is small. What is the best next move?

options.text: Lower that weight a little, because the gradient says pushing it up would increase the loss.
Raise that weight a lot, because positive numbers always mean 'add more.'
Delete the whole layer and start over.
Ignore the gradient and keep the weight unchanged forever.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The gradient warns that increasing this weight would make the error worse, so the optimizer nudges the weight downward by a small amount.
